elements.sent-2411-059: By a (rational) lattice, we mean a free abelian group $L$ of finite rank equipped with a nondegenerate bilinear form $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle\colon L\times L\to\Q$.
elements.sent-2411-060: The lattice $L$ is called integral if $\langle v,w\rangle\in\Z$ for all $v,w\in L$.
elements.sent-2411-061: If $\langle v,v\rangle\in 2\Z$ for all $v\in L$, then $L$ is called even.
elements.sent-2411-062: An integral lattice that is not even is called odd.
elements.sent-2411-063: Given a lattice $L$, we denote by $L^*=\{v\in L\otimes_\Z\Q\mid\langle v,w\rangle\in\Z\text{ for all w\in L\}$ the dual of $L$, embedded via $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ into the ambient space of $L$.}
elements.sent-2411-064: If $L$ is even, then $L\subset L^*$ and the quotient $L^*\!/L$ is a finite, abelian group endowed with a nondegenerate quadratic form $Q\colon L^*\!/L\to\C^\times$ given by $Q(v+L)=\e(\langle v,v\rangle/2)$ for all $v\in L^*$.
elements.sent-2411-065: In that situation, we call $L^*\!/L$ the discriminant form of $L$.
elements.sent-2411-066: More generally, we call any pair $(\Gamma,Q)$ of a finite, abelian group $\Gamma$ together with a nondegenerate quadratic form $Q\colon\Gamma\to\C^\times$ a discriminant form (sometimes called metric group).
elements.sent-2411-067: The function $Q$ being quadratic means that $\smash{Q(na)=Q(a)^{n^2 }$ for all $a\in\Gamma$ and $n\in\Z$ and that $B_Q\colon\Gamma\times\Gamma \to\C^\times$ with $B_Q(a,b)\coloneqq Q(a+b)Q(a)^{-1 Q(b)^{-1}$ for $a,b\in\Gamma$ is bimultiplicative (and nondegenerate).}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We call $B_Q$ the associated bimultiplicative form.
elements.sent-2411-069: Any discriminant form $(\Gamma,Q)$ can be realised as dual quotient $L^*\!/L$ for some even lattice~$L$ \cite{Nik80 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: Given two discriminant forms $(\Gamma_1,Q_1)$ and $(\Gamma_2,Q_2)$, we denote their (orthogonal) direct sum by $(\Gamma_1 \oplus \Gamma_2, Q_1 \oplus Q_2)$, which represents the group $\Gamma_1 \oplus \Gamma_2$ with the quadratic form $ Q_1 \oplus Q_2 $ defined by $ (Q_1 \oplus Q_2)((a_1, a_2)) = Q_1(a_1)Q_2(a_2) $.
elements.sent-2411-071: A discriminant form is called indecomposable if it cannot be written as an orthogonal direct sum of two nonzero discriminant forms.
